name: Excelsior Mountain
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding on gentle slopes of Excelsior Mountain. Hunt for quartz, agate, and jasper specimens in this accessible Mineral County location with no permit required.
description:
Excelsior Mountain sits in the heart of Nevada's mineral-rich Mineral County on public BLM land open to recreational collectors. The gentle slopes at 6,172 feet elevation make this an accessible destination for rockhounds of all experience levels seeking quartz varieties, agate, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. The site benefits from Nevada's famous high desert geology - Miocene-era volcanic and hydrothermal activity deposited silica-rich fluids through fractures and cavities in rhyolite and andesite rock, creating the colorful quartz aggregates and banded agates collectors find here today.

The terrain features gentle slopes with scattered outcrops and loose material on hillsides and washes. Most collectors focus on surface collecting around visible rock formations and in the sandy/gravelly drainages where specimens weather out naturally. The 6,172-foot elevation provides comfortable temperatures during spring and fall months, though afternoon thunderstorms are possible June through September.

This location is ideal for family groups and beginners because access is straightforward, no permits or fees apply, and the gentle slopes don't require technical hiking. You are limited to 25 pounds per day under FLPMA regulations, which is plenty for quality specimens. The nearby Mineral County area has seen intermittent small-scale prospecting and mining activity since the 1800s, though Excelsior Mountain itself has remained primarily a rockhounding destination rather than a commercial mine.

Bring sturdy hiking boots for the uneven rocky terrain, plenty of water (the area is remote and exposed), and containers to carry your finds. Cell service can be spotty this far from town, so plan your trip carefully and let someone know your location.
history: Excelsior Mountain lies within the Basin and Range province where Miocene volcanic activity 20-30 million years ago deposited silica-rich hydrothermal fluids through fractures in rhyolite and andesite host rocks, creating the quartz veins, chalcedony deposits, and agate-filled cavities found here today. The Mineral County region has a long history of small-scale mining and prospecting dating to the 1800s, and while Excelsior Mountain was never developed as a major mine, it remains a productive rockhounding site where surface specimens continue to weather out of the volcanic bedrock.
tips: Search the gentle hillsides and dry washes on the lower slopes where agates and quartz specimens naturally weather out of the host rock - focus on areas with visible light-colored outcrops and concentrations of loose rock. Use a classifier or sifter if you're searching gravelly washes, and bring a small pry bar to work loose specimens from weathered outcrops without causing damage. Look for banded or striped agate patterns ranging from white and gray to rust tones, clear to milky quartz crystals, and jasper with red, brown, or cream coloring - the best specimens often have good color saturation and visible banding.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6,172 feet on gentle to moderate slopes with scattered volcanic rock outcrops. Terrain is uneven and rocky with loose material common on hillsides and in dry washes. Vegetation is sparse high desert scrub - sage brush and low shrubs. Walking distance from parking varies depending on access point but most productive areas are within 0.5 to 2 miles. Footing is firm on sandy/gravelly areas but requires attention to loose rocks on steeper sections.
best_season: April - May and September - October. Spring offers mild temperatures and the desert wildflower bloom, while early fall provides cool mornings and low humidity. Avoid June - August when afternoon thunderstorms are common and heat can be intense at this elevation. Winter (November - March) is possible but nights drop below freezing and roads may become impassable after snow.
written_directions: From Hawthorne (the nearest town, approximately 30 miles southwest), take US 95 north toward Mina. Continue on US 95 for about 20 miles north of Hawthorne. Look for Forest Road 024 or local access roads heading east into the Excelsior Mountain area near the Mineral County line. Follow BLM access roads as conditions allow - check current conditions locally as seasonal washout is possible. GPS coordinates 38.329028, -118.339084 mark the approximate center of the collecting area. High-clearance vehicle recommended for rougher BLM roads; passenger vehicles can access nearby areas via maintained roads.
road_conditions: Rough BLM dirt roads, possible washboard and ruts. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Some roads may be impassable in winter or after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along BLM access roads. No developed parking area. Park safely off the road to avoid blocking access.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or removal of material with heavy equipment. Collect only loose specimens and surface material - do not break open live rock or damage outcrops. Pack out all trash and leave the site in good condition.

nearest_services: Hawthorne, Nevada, approximately 30 miles south. Mina, Nevada, approximately 25 miles north. Both towns have gas, food, and basic supplies. Bring plenty of water as there are no services at the site.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain accessible to most hikers. Uneven rocky footing and loose material require sturdy hiking boots. No wheelchair access due to rough BLM roads and uneven terrain. Most collecting areas are within easy walking distance of parking. Not suitable for those with significant mobility limitations.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding at Excelsior Mountain near Mineral County, Nevada?
faq.answer: Excelsior Mountain is known for quartz varieties, agate with banding and color, chalcedony, and jasper in red, brown, and cream tones. Most specimens are found as loose surface material in washes and weathered out of volcanic rock outcrops on the gentle hillsides.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect at Excelsior Mountain, Nevada?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Excelsior Mountain - it sits on public BLM land open under standard personal use regulations. You can collect free of charge up to 25 pounds per day.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Excelsior Mountain?
faq.answer: April through May and September through October are ideal. Spring offers mild temperatures and wildflowers, while early fall provides cool mornings. Avoid summer (June-August) due to afternoon thunderstorms and heat, and winter roads can become impassable.

faq.question: Is Excelsior Mountain rockhounding site suitable for beginners and families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes - the gentle slopes, no-permit requirement, and free access make Excelsior Mountain excellent for beginners and families. Kids ages 6 and up can participate, and the 25-pound daily limit is plenty for collecting quality specimens. No special skills are needed for surface collecting.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need to collect agate and quartz at Excelsior Mountain, Nevada?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots for uneven rocky terrain, a small pry bar or chisel to work loose specimens, a classifier or sifter for wash material, collection bags or containers, and most importantly, plenty of water. Cell service is spotty, so bring a map and GPS unit or mark your location.

faq.question: How far is Excelsior Mountain from the nearest town with services?
faq.answer: Hawthorne, Nevada is approximately 30 miles south and has gas, food, and basic supplies. Mina is about 25 miles north. There are no services at the site itself, so fill up fuel and water before heading to Excelsior Mountain.
